overview.problem: c-monitor needs dual-provider monitoring for Claude and OpenAI Codex while lowering runtime memory usage and preserving existing claude-monitor workflows.
overview.approach: Execute a measured-first migration: establish provider adapters behind existing CLI interfaces, optimize ingestion and caching for bounded memory, and gate any language rewrite on benchmark thresholds.

decisions.decision: Use measured Python optimization before any language rewrite
decisions.reasoning: Provider support is urgent and compatibility-sensitive -> streaming parse and bounded caches deliver near-term memory wins with low migration risk -> rewrite only after metrics miss explicit targets

decisions.decision: Introduce provider adapters with a normalized UsageEntry contract
decisions.reasoning: Claude and Codex telemetry schemas differ in paths and token keys -> adapter boundary isolates source variance -> analyzer and UI stay provider-agnostic

decisions.decision: Adopt streaming JSONL ingestion with bounded dedupe state
decisions.reasoning: Full raw history materialization scales linearly with file size -> line streaming with capped hash windows keeps working set bounded -> rss_peak remains stable during long sessions

decisions.decision: Use short TTL monitoring cache plus bounded projection memoization
decisions.reasoning: Realtime refresh repeatedly requests similar aggregates -> tiny TTL cache and bounded projection memoization remove redundant recomputation -> CPU and memory stay predictable

decisions.decision: Keep claude_monitor package path and legacy command aliases through migration
decisions.reasoning: Existing scripts and habits depend on current entrypoints -> preserving module path and aliases avoids workflow breakage -> provider refactor can ship incrementally

decisions.decision: Gate optional Go or Rust rewrite on benchmark thresholds
decisions.reasoning: Rewrite cost and backtrack cost are high -> explicit rss_peak rss_p95 and startup thresholds make the decision objective -> language migration triggers only when optimized Python misses gate

decisions.decision: Prioritize integration fixtures over mock-heavy tests for provider rollout
decisions.reasoning: Provider regressions emerge at parser and orchestration boundaries -> fixture-driven integration tests observe end-to-end behavior -> targeted unit tests remain for token normalization and pricing math

rejected_alternatives.alternative: Immediate full language rewrite to Go or Rust
rejected_alternatives.rejection_reason: Migration and compatibility risk remains high before measuring optimized Python baseline
Unbounded parsing and caching behavior cannot meet memory budget targets

constraints: MUST: use planning workflow output after project setup
MUST: incorporate deep reasoning and critical evaluation before final recommendation
SHOULD: preserve compatibility with existing claude-monitor workflows during migration
SHOULD: base language and framework decisions on measured memory and performance outcomes

risks.risk: Codex transcript variants break parser assumptions and silently drop usage data
risks.mitigation: Use fixture matrix across provider variants and enforce normalization tests in CI

risks.risk: Memory optimizations regress realtime display latency or compatibility behavior
risks.mitigation: Track latency and compatibility regressions in integration tests before release

risks.risk: Benchmark gate remains ambiguous and blocks release decisions
risks.mitigation: Publish explicit rss_peak rss_p95 and startup thresholds with go and no-go criteria

invisible_knowledge.system: Provider adapters isolate ingestion variability so session analysis and UI layers operate on a stable schema while bounded caches protect memory budget under continuous refresh.
invariants: Reader and adapter paths emit canonical UsageEntry fields before analysis.
Compatibility aliases continue to launch the same entrypoint during migration.
Monitoring caches remain bounded and observable through telemetry counters.
tradeoffs: Measured optimization in Python ships faster and lowers migration risk before any rewrite.
Provider abstraction adds interface overhead but localizes schema drift and test scope.
Fixture-heavy integration tests increase setup effort but reduce cross-layer regression risk.

milestones.name: Bootstrap provider contract and compatibility surface
requirements: Expose provider selection with claude default
Preserve existing command aliases and module path contracts
Define normalized provider adapter interface for downstream analysis
acceptance_criteria: Default startup without provider flag preserves Claude monitoring behavior
Provider codex selection resolves provider paths without runtime failure
Repository metadata and docs state c-monitor fork goals and compatibility guarantees
tests: integration: cli startup default and provider codex fixture paths
unit: settings validation for provider values and persistence
regression: alias commands map to unchanged entrypoint

code_intents.behavior: Add provider and provider_data_path settings with strict validation and last-used persistence while keeping claude as default.
Resolve provider-specific default roots and route the selected provider into monitoring initialization without changing existing command aliases.
Expose adapter lookup for claude and codex providers and return an iterator contract that emits normalized records.
Document provider flag usage and migration compatibility commitments for existing claude-monitor workflows.
Keep script aliases and package metadata aligned with c-monitor naming and dual-provider positioning.
Capture measurable objectives and migration guardrails for provider support and memory budget execution.

milestones.name: Build Codex ingestion and normalization pipeline
requirements: Parse Codex and Claude transcripts into shared UsageEntry fields
Normalize token and model fields across provider schema variants
Extend pricing behavior for codex models with deterministic fallback
acceptance_criteria: Mixed provider fixtures generate stable blocks and totals in analyze_usage
Cached and calculated cost modes remain valid for Claude workflows
Unknown provider model names do not crash and surface fallback behavior
tests: integration: analyze_usage with mixed provider fixtures
unit: token extractor matrix for claude and codex keys
unit: pricing calculator codex mapping and fallback paths

code_intents.behavior: Stream provider records through adapters and emit normalized UsageEntry rows without retaining full raw history in memory.
Recognize claude and codex token key variants and return canonical input output and cache token counts.
Map normalized codex models to pricing tables and preserve deterministic fallback behavior for unknown names.
Build session blocks from normalized provider entries while minimizing duplicate raw-entry collections.
Retain Claude limit detection semantics and isolate provider-specific limit parsing hooks.
Add mixed provider fixture coverage for parsing and dedupe behavior.
Verify end-to-end block generation and totals from mixed provider datasets.
Cover codex model pricing and fallback pathways alongside existing Claude cases.

milestones.name: Enforce memory budget and runtime observability
requirements: Cap in-process cache growth under continuous refresh loops
Collect rss_peak and rss_p95 metrics for representative workloads
Keep realtime and table output fields stable for Claude compatibility
acceptance_criteria: Long-running monitor loop maintains bounded cache state
Benchmark fixture run records rss_peak and rss_p95 below target threshold
Realtime output preserves existing key fields and notifications
tests: integration: orchestrator long-run fixture with bounded cache assertions
benchmark: memory profile scenario with reproducible command
regression: display parity tests for realtime and table views

code_intents.behavior: Bound cache payload retention and expose cache-age metrics that support memory telemetry.
Aggregate from streaming or iterator inputs without duplicate list materialization for long histories.
Use bounded memoization keyed by workload slice so custom limit computation remains memory stable.
Emit fetch and memory telemetry while preserving robust fallback behavior during transient parse failures.
Render from aggregated monitoring payloads without duplicating large entry structures in UI assembly.
Assert bounded cache behavior and telemetry emission in repeated monitoring cycles.
Confirm realtime rendering parity and notification behavior after memory optimizations.
Validate provider routing and alias compatibility in startup flows.

milestones.name: Define migration gate and operating playbook
requirements: Document benchmark workloads and pass fail thresholds for rss metrics
Publish provider compatibility matrix with fallback semantics
Define go no-go gate for optional language migration
acceptance_criteria: Migration decision criteria cite explicit measured thresholds
Contributor guide includes reproducible benchmark and compatibility commands
Release notes capture fork baseline and rollout checkpoints
tests: docs-check: markdown links and commands validated in CI smoke flow
integration: api examples run for claude and codex fixture inputs

code_intents.behavior: Specify workload matrix and rss_peak rss_p95 thresholds that gate rewrite decisions.
List supported provider fields and fallback mappings for claude and codex ingestion.
Document benchmark and compatibility command sequence required before release.
Capture fork baseline and phased delivery checkpoints for provider and memory milestones.
Provide fixture-driven usage examples for both provider modes and memory benchmark hooks.
